rule: NEVER close an incident or declare a bug resolved without explicit confirmation from the user that the fix works in production/live testing. Code compiling and tests passing is NOT the same as the bug being fixed. Only the operator who reported the bug can confirm resolution.
context: INC-001: Attempted to close incident after M1-M3 passed tests, but live testnet immediately revealed a new deadlock bug (RC-4A). User corrected: the bug is not solved until they say it is.

rule: When debugging cascading failures, do not assume each fix will resolve the problem. Trace the FULL causal chain to the absolute bottom before proposing fixes. In this case, 7 consecutive fix attempts addressed symptoms and intermediate causes but the true root (gossip propagation exceeding fallback window) was only found after exhaustive tracing through production logs showing competing blocks at the very first slot.
context: INC-001: 7 surgical fixes applied across 5 hours, each fixing a real bug but none resolving the user-visible symptom. The root cause was architectural: the 2s fallback window assumes faster block propagation than reality.

rule: When a fix changes behavior in one subsystem (e.g., production scheduling), immediately audit ALL code paths that must agree on the same data (e.g., validation). A unilateral change creates a silent mismatch that manifests as intermittent failures. RC-5A fixed production but not validation — the mismatch caused 80% block rejection for 10 root-cause sessions before discovery.
context: INC-001 RC-10: Production used hardcoded genesis producers, validation used divergent GSet. Fix was obvious once found but took 10 sessions because symptoms (forks, rollback loops, empty slots) pointed away from the validation path.

rule: When diagnosing consensus fork cascades, check for asymmetric transformations between production and validation scheduler inputs. The DeterministicScheduler is deterministic -- non-determinism comes from its INPUTS. Any LOCAL state (HashMap, cache, wall clock) used to modify scheduler inputs on the production side but not replicated in validation creates a guaranteed fork when the modification changes a value. Compare exact code paths that build scheduler input in both paths.
context: INC-003: inactivity_leak (mod.rs:155-184) and emergency_equalization (mod.rs:239-277) applied production-only, validation uses raw bond weights. Fork cascade after ~3hrs.

rule: Before diagnosing any distributed system failure, FIRST verify the system is operating within its designed parameters: node count vs max_peers, resource usage (RAM, CPU, disk I/O, file descriptors), network topology constraints, and whether the deployment configuration matches what the code was tested for. A 50-node network on one machine is a capacity question, not a consensus bug. Ask the elemental question first.
context: INC-005: Spent 3 sessions diagnosing fork cascades that were ultimately caused by running 55 processes on one machine with aggressive fork recovery. Never once checked if 50 nodes was within design parameters until the user asked. The answer took 2 minutes to find.

rule: When restoring files from a historical git snapshot to recover lost work, ALWAYS diff the restored files against the current HEAD to verify no later fixes are silently reverted. A merge regression that reverts a known fix is the most dangerous class of bug because it reintroduces a problem that was already diagnosed and solved. Commit b0b7c18 restored INC-001 fixes from snapshot 84e7ffa1 but silently reverted INC-003/INC-004 fixes, causing the same fork cascade to recur.
context: INC-005: merge regression reverted INC-003/INC-004 fixes

rule: Open an INC ticket IMMEDIATELY when the user reports a bug — before any investigation. The incident exists at symptom-report time, not at root-cause-confirmation time. A workflow_run is not a substitute for an incident ticket. The INC tracks the bug lifecycle; the workflow_run tracks the agent work. Both must exist from the start.
context: INC-006: User reported chain stall with FORK/integrity failures. Registered workflow_run but did NOT open an INC until after full diagnosis + fix implementation. User had to remind me. The protocol says every bug gets a ticket — that means at report time, not after the fix.

rule: When a skill/protocol loads with numbered steps, execute them IN ORDER. Do not cherry-pick. Do not skip steps because they feel like overhead. Do not substitute manual work for the prescribed agent invocations. The protocol is a checklist, not a suggestion. Specifically: INC creation happens at symptom-report time (step 0), not after diagnosis. Urgency makes protocols MORE important, not less.
context: INC-006: /omega-diagnose loaded with explicit steps (register workflow, create INC, fundamentals gate, invoke diagnostician). I registered the workflow but skipped INC creation, skipped fundamentals gate, skipped diagnostician agent. Went freestyle for 45+ minutes of investigation. Root cause: treated the skill as context/intent rather than a strict protocol. Urgency bias made it worse — I prioritized evidence-gathering over process.

rule: Before proposing a recovery/repair mechanism, ask: is full block history integrity even necessary for the node role? In PoS blockchains, state roots ARE the integrity proof. Producing nodes need correct state + recent blocks, not a verified copy of every historical block. Archival is a separate concern from consensus participation. Do not propose O(chain_height) solutions when O(1) state root verification is sufficient.
context: INC-006: Proposed block-by-block integrity repair (O(chain_height) walk + peer comparison) without questioning whether it is necessary or scalable at thousands of nodes. The user correctly challenged: state roots already prove integrity. Block history is archival, not consensus-critical.

rule: Before proposing any solution, first ask: does this problem need solving? Then ask: at what scale? If the answer is a mechanism that grows with data size (O(n)) when a constant-time proof already exists (O(1)), STOP. The over-engineered solution is worse than no solution — it wastes time, erodes trust, and introduces complexity that itself becomes a liability. Propose the simplest verification boundary that already exists before inventing new ones.
context: Proposed O(chain_height) block-by-block integrity repair when O(1) state root verification already solves the problem. User called it over-engineered. Pattern: jumping to implementation without evaluating necessity or existing primitives.
